skill.name: mcp-builder
skill.description: Guide for building MCP servers in TypeScript or Python with tool design, transports, testing, and evaluation guidance.
skill.summary: Build practical MCP servers with implementation patterns, quality checks, and evaluation workflows.
skill.author: Azeem-2

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, credential, and weak-crypto patterns. Review found no prompt injection or malicious intent; most flagged markdown lines are documentation examples, while the bundled evaluation scripts legitimately run user-selected MCP servers and forward user-provided credentials.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled MCP Server Execution
medium_findings.description: The evaluation harness accepts a command and arguments for stdio transport, then creates a subprocess connection to that MCP server. This is legitimate for testing but risky with untrusted commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command path is explicit and semantically confirmed by the stdio connection factory. It is intended functionality, so the risk depends on whether users supply trusted commands.

medium_findings.title: Remote MCP Connections With User-Provided Headers
medium_findings.description: The harness accepts remote SSE or HTTP URLs and arbitrary headers. This can expose tokens to the selected MCP endpoint if users configure an untrusted server.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code and documentation both support remote URLs with Authorization headers. This is normal MCP evaluation behavior, but users must trust the endpoint.

medium_findings.title: Credential Forwarding For Test Servers
medium_findings.description: The scripts parse environment variables and pass them to the selected stdio MCP server. The examples include API keys and GitHub tokens, so users may expose secrets to tested servers.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Credential parsing and forwarding are directly visible, and the examples show token-style variables. The behavior is user-directed rather than hidden exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Markdown Examples Trigger Command Pattern Alerts
low_findings.description: Many static command hits occur inside documentation fences or prose examples. They describe build, install, and evaluation commands rather than hidden execution by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is documentation or template text, not runtime code. It explains expected developer commands and project structure.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography alerts match ordinary words, markdown syntax, examples, or schema terminology. No evidence found of cryptographic hashing or encryption in executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed samples show guidance text and SDK descriptions, not crypto APIs. The scanner appears to match short tokens inside markdown.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to MCP documentation, SDK README files, or placeholder example endpoints. No evidence found of covert network destinations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible instructional references and example values. They are not embedded in hidden data transfer logic.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess-Based stdio Transport
dangerous_patterns.description: The harness can start a user-specified command for stdio MCP testing. Run it only with trusted local server commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess-style stdio parameters are directly constructed from command, args, and env. This is expected for MCP stdio servers.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Configured Network Transport
dangerous_patterns.description: The harness supports SSE and streamable HTTP URLs with custom headers. Use only trusted endpoints and avoid sending secrets to unknown servers.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network transport classes clearly forward URL and header values. This is legitimate connection code with endpoint trust risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment Variable Forwarding
dangerous_patterns.description: The harness can forward user-entered environment variables to a tested stdio server. Treat those values as secrets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The parser converts KEY=VALUE inputs into a dictionary used for the connection. No hidden collection was found.

content.user_title: Build MCP Servers With Guided Patterns
content.value_statement: MCP server projects need clear tool design, transport choices, and reliable evaluations. This skill provides structured guidance for planning, implementing, testing, and evaluating MCP integrations.
actual_capabilities: Explains MCP server planning, tool naming, and workflow-oriented API coverage.
Provides TypeScript MCP SDK implementation guidance with Zod schemas and transports.
Provides Python FastMCP implementation guidance with Pydantic models and async patterns.
Includes best practices for response formats, pagination, annotations, and error handling.
Includes an evaluation workflow and scripts for testing MCP tools with realistic questions.
Points users to official MCP and SDK documentation for deeper protocol details.
limitations: It is guidance and scaffolding, not a complete generated MCP server for a specific service.
Users must verify current MCP SDK APIs before relying on copied examples.
The evaluation harness can run trusted local commands or connect to trusted remote servers only.
It does not replace a security review of the MCP server that users build.

use_cases.title: Plan a new MCP integration
use_cases.description: Map an external service API into useful MCP tools, response formats, and transport choices.

use_cases.title: Implement production-ready tool handlers
use_cases.description: Use TypeScript or Python patterns for validation, error handling, pagination, and structured output.

use_cases.title: Evaluate server usefulness
use_cases.description: Create realistic read-only questions and run them against an MCP server to measure agent success.

prompt_templates.title: Plan the server
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a new API integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder skill to plan an MCP server for [service]. Identify tool groups, auth needs, response formats, and the best transport.

prompt_templates.title: Design tool schemas
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder skill to design MCP tools for [workflow]. Include names, inputs, annotations, pagination, and error messages.

prompt_templates.title: Implement core server
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder skill to implement a [TypeScript or Python] MCP server for [service]. Follow the recommended SDK patterns and validation approach.

prompt_templates.title: Create evaluations
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder skill to create ten read-only MCP evaluation questions for [server]. Make each answer stable, verifiable, and realistic.

output_examples.input: Plan an MCP server for a project management API.
output_examples.output: A structured implementation plan with tool groups for projects, tasks, users, comments, pagination, authentication, and transport selection.

output_examples.input: Review my MCP tool list for quality gaps.
output: Renamed vague tools with service prefixes.
Added pagination guidance for list tools.
Marked destructive actions with appropriate annotations.

output_examples.input: Create an evaluation set for a GitHub MCP server.
output_examples.output: A set of read-only questions that test multi-step repository, issue, pull request, and contributor workflows with stable answers.

best_practices: Prefer clear service-prefixed tool names with narrow, accurate descriptions.
Use validation schemas, pagination, structured output, and actionable errors for every tool.
Run evaluation scripts only against trusted local commands or trusted remote MCP servers.
anti_patterns: Do not expose broad generic tools when focused workflow tools would be clearer.
Do not pass API keys, headers, or environment variables to untrusted MCP servers.
Do not rely only on annotations for security decisions or destructive action controls.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps you design, implement, and evaluate MCP servers that connect AI assistants to external services.

faq.question: Does it support TypeScript and Python?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for the TypeScript MCP SDK and Python FastMCP patterns.

faq.question: Can it generate a complete server automatically?
faq.answer: It provides implementation guidance and examples. You still need to adapt the server to your service API.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The included evaluation harness can run user-selected commands and forward credentials. This is useful but requires trusted inputs.

faq.question: Should I use the bundled evaluation scripts?
faq.answer: Use them in a controlled environment with trusted MCP servers, trusted URLs, and limited credentials.

faq.question: Is this safe for marketplace publication?
faq.answer: Yes, with warnings. No malicious intent was found, but users must understand command, network, and credential risks.
